phrases.cultural_note: A classic French arrival greeting — casual and warm. Add 'e' at the end if you're a woman: 'rentrée'.

phrases.text_local: Ça sent vraiment bon ! Qu'est-ce qu'on mange ce soir ?
phrases.pronunciation: sah son vray-MON bon! kess-kon manzh suh SWAHR
phrases.cultural_note: Complimenting the cook is always welcome. This phrase combines a compliment with a question — very natural French.
French pet commands are simple and direct: 'viens' (come), 'assis' (sit), 'reste' (stay), 'couché' (lie down). Pets respond to tone as much as words.

phrases.text_local: Où est-elle ? Je ne la vois pas.
phrases.pronunciation: oo eh-TEL? zhuh nuh lah vwah PAH
phrases.cultural_note: 'Je ne la vois pas' (I don't see her) uses the direct object pronoun 'la' — a great everyday structure. Use 'il' and 'le' for a male subject.

responses.french: Elle est dans le jardin, je crois.
responses.english: She's in the garden, I think.
responses.phonetic: el eh don luh zhar-DAN, zhuh KRWAH

phrases.intent_en: Say it's time for the dog's walk
phrases.text_local: C'est l'heure de promener le chien !
phrases.pronunciation: say luhr duh prom-NAY luh SHYAN
phrases.cultural_note: 'Promener le chien' (walk the dog) is the go-to French phrase. You can also say 'sortir le chien' — either is perfectly natural.

phrases.intent_en: Share that you had a great day
phrases.text_local: J'ai eu une excellente journée. Et toi ?
phrases.pronunciation: zhay ew un ex-uh-LONT zhoor-NAY. ay TWAH?
phrases.cultural_note: Sharing your day with 'Et toi ?' (And you?) invites the conversation back. A very French social habit.

responses.french: Moi aussi, ça s'est bien passé !
responses.phonetic: mwah oh-SEE, sah say byan pah-SAY

phrases.text_local: Tu veux un verre de vin ?
phrases.pronunciation: tew vuh uh vair duh van
phrases.cultural_note: 'Un verre de vin' is the most natural everyday way to offer wine casually at home.

phrases.intent_en: Tell the dogs to settle down
phrases.cultural_note: 'Couché' means lie down for a dog. 'Sages' means well-behaved or calm. Both are great everyday command words.

phrases.intent_en: Suggest drinking wine on the outdoor deck
phrases.text_local: On boit un verre de vin sur la terrasse ?
phrases.pronunciation: on bwah un vair duh van sewr lah teh-RASS
phrases.cultural_note: 'Sur la terrasse' is a very natural phrase for enjoying a drink outside. 'On boit... ?' sounds relaxed and inclusive, like 'Shall we have...?'

phrases.text_local: On prend une pizza pour le dîner ?
phrases.pronunciation: on pron un PEET-zah poor luh dee-NAY
phrases.cultural_note: 'On prend...' is a common colloquial way to suggest getting food together. 'Pour le dîner' keeps it clearly about dinner.

responses.english: Yes, that sounds great to me!
